Safety risk assessment, accident analysi.claim_text: The study advances understanding of safety science principles in organizational contexts
Research addresses factors contributing to workplace safety and accident prevention
The paper contributes to risk assessment methodologies in safety management

pedestrian_red_light_running_simulation.claim_text: The model successfully represents the two types of pedestrian behavior (law-obeying and opportunistic) at signalized intersections in Chinese cities

Using calibrated parameters from questionnaires (r1=0.55, r2=0.69) produces red light runner proportions (mean 0.69) lower than field observations (mean 0.85), while adjusting to r1=0.35 brings simulation results closer to field data

The model captures violation follower behavior where pedestrians of Type I follow others to violate traffic signals within 2 seconds, with simulation results closely matching field observations (mean 0.19 vs 0.18)
External factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' violation behavior, and traffic volume significantly influence pedestrian signal non-compliance decisions in Chinese cities

The gap acceptance criterion ΔT calculated from pedestrian's estimated vehicle arrival time provides a practical measure for pedestrians deciding whether to cross or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows

gap_acceptance_modeling.claim_text: The model successfully represents the two types of pedestrian behavior (law-obeying and opportunistic) at signalized intersections in Chinese cities

Using calibrated parameters from questionnaires (r1=0.55, r2=0.69) produces red light runner proportions (mean 0.69) lower than field observations (mean 0.85), while adjusting to r1=0.35 brings simulation results closer to field data

The model captures violation follower behavior where pedestrians of Type I follow others to violate traffic signals within 2 seconds, with simulation results closely matching field observations (mean 0.19 vs 0.18)
External factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' violation behavior, and traffic volume significantly influence pedestrian signal non-compliance decisions in Chinese cities

The gap acceptance criterion ΔT calculated from pedestrian's estimated vehicle arrival time provides a practical measure for pedestrians deciding whether to cross or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows

The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with simulated red light runner proportions (0.82 mean) matching field observations (0.85 mean) after parameter adjustment

Classifying pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions based on external factors (policeman, other pedestrians' behavior, vehicle flow) provides more accurate simulation than fixed proportions
The gap acceptance criterion DT calculated from pedestrian-estimated vehicle velocity better represents real pedestrian decision-making compared to methods using actual vehicle instantaneous speed
Questionnaire calibration alone produces discrepancies (red light jumper proportion 0.69 vs field 0.85), requiring adjustment based on analyzing deviation causes to achieve better agreement

The gap acceptance criterion ΔT calculated from pedestrian's estimated vehicle arrival time provides a practical measure for pedestrians deciding whether to cross or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows

pedestrian_trajectory_estimation.claim_text: The modified social force model can accurately reproduce pedestrian trajectory with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
The model successfully represents lane-formation phenomenon with number of formed lanes (3.5) closer to observed (2.8) compared to non-route-choice model (4.1)
The model can represent pedestrian collision avoidance behavior with turning vehicles, with passing position shifting from 0 to 2-2.5m when conflicts exist
Separating parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups for calibration is an effective approach achieving favorable calibration results
The desired direction model using Weibull distribution with influencing factors (crosswalk geometry, OD movement, pedestrian density) reasonably reflects pedestrian behavior characteristics

pedestrian_crossing_behavior_at_crosswal.claim_text: Pedestrians receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and braking text were the most favored visual interfaces while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAVs poorly even with external features
Pedestrians who intentionally violate traffic rules or get distracted became more cautious with FAVs and appreciated external features

Predict pedestrian violating road-crossi.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention across all four models (p<0.001 in all cases)
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI=0.996, CMIN/df=1.235, RMSEA=0.030
Subjective norm was significant in Model 1 (basic TPB) but became non-significant after descriptive norm was added, indicating descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior (p=0.004 in Model 4), indicating that presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention
Perceived behavioral control was not a significant predictor in any model, likely because none of the respondents were physically impaired and the violation behavior was not challenging enough

pedestrian crossing task at signalized i.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and then music distraction
All three mobile phone distractions cause pedestrians to initiate crossing later, increase pupil diameter, and reduce scanning frequency and fixation points/times toward traffic signal area
Phone conversation distraction leads to slower crossing speed, fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and less fixation time and average fixation duration on left traffic area
Text distraction causes pedestrians to look left and right less often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment
Eye movement data captured by eye tracker is an effective complement to video data for determining individuals visual attention allocation more accurately

analyze_pedestrian_red_light_violation_b.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649)
Pedestrian age significantly affects red light running propensity: adolescents have lower (b=-0.228) and elderly have higher propensity (b=0.174) compared to adults
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (b=-0.984, odds ratio=0.374)
Social influences strongly affect individual violation decisions: presence of other violators increases propensity (b=1.029) while more pedestrians waiting reduces it (b=-0.394)
Environmental factors including time to green, red time, traffic volume, and number of lanes significantly affect red light running propensity

Pedestrian waiting time modeling.claim_text: The U-shaped distribution of pedestrian intended waiting times emerges from the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians via statistical unconditioning, showing that a large proportion of pedestrians cross immediately while another large proportion wait for the entire red-man phase

The proposed bounded Pareto mixture model better captures pedestrian waiting time behavior at signalized intersections compared to conventional exponential distribution and its extensions, which cannot adequately model the bounded nature and U-shaped pattern of waiting times
Risk-taking pedestrians have significantly smaller intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians due to their lower effective critical headway and higher sensitivity to excess headway

Younger pedestrians tend to be risk-takers (higher proportion cross immediately) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (higher proportion wait for entire phase); males are more aggressive than females in crossing behavior

Identify significant factors affecting p.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians going to work/school have higher red-light running probability than strolling pedestrians (odds ratio = 0.394 for stroll group)
Daytime period shows the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, dusk, and night (OR = 3.326 for daytime vs dawn)
Pedestrians who indicate they will run red lights when in hurry have significantly higher probability of red-light running (odds ratio = 0.299)
Pedestrians who are affected by road facility quality (imperfect facilities) are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio = 0.483 for unaffected group)

analyze_pedestrian_crossing_behavior.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity to run red lights compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
Elderly pedestrians have higher red light running propensity than adults, contrary to some previous findings
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (odds ratio = 0.579), and presence of other violators increases it (odds ratio = 4.023)
Traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles are negatively associated with red light running propensity
Significant interaction effects exist between gender and traffic volume, gender and other violators, elderly and companion status on red light running propensity

modeling_vehicle_pedestrian_interaction.claim_text: The proposed model successfully describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, with validation showing maximum relative error less than 10% between simulated and surveyed pedestrian waiting times

Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of traffic lanes and vehicle volume but decreases with pedestrian volume; the number of lanes is the critical factor affecting pedestrian waiting time at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Vehicular delay increases more rapidly than pedestrian waiting time with increasing vehicle volume and pedestrian volume, due to queue formation and platoon departure behavior

Signal control is recommended when vehicular volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h, using 20 seconds delay as LOS B threshold; unsignalized crosswalks should not be set on segments with more than 4 lanes

Pedestrian crossing decision recognition.claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system correctly recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation across different noise levels (rn = 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 m)
The system recognizes wait decisions with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds of observation, taking longer than cross decisions due to deceleration cues
The proposed probabilistic model provides confidence scores (probabilities) for states rather than binary classifications, enabling varied vehicle behaviors based on confidence levels
The DBN model successfully integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian physical states, mimicking human decision-making processes
Walking motion recognition maintains 90% accuracy across noise levels, while running motion recognition is more challenging due to overlapping speed distributions with walking

Using calibrated parameters from questionnaires (r1=0.55, r2=0.69) produces red light runner proportions (mean 0.69) lower than field observations (mean 0.85), while adjusting to r1=0.35 brings simulation results closer to field data

The model captures violation follower behavior where pedestrians of Type I follow others to violate traffic signals within 2 seconds, with simulation results closely matching field observations (mean 0.19 vs 0.18)
External factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' violation behavior, and traffic volume significantly influence pedestrian signal non-compliance decisions in Chinese cities

The gap acceptance criterion ΔT calculated from pedestrian's estimated vehicle arrival time provides a practical measure for pedestrians deciding whether to cross or wait when conflicting with vehicle flows

The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with simulated red light runner proportions (0.82 mean) matching field observations (0.85 mean) after parameter adjustment

Classifying pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions based on external factors (policeman, other pedestrians' behavior, vehicle flow) provides more accurate simulation than fixed proportions
The gap acceptance criterion DT calculated from pedestrian-estimated vehicle velocity better represents real pedestrian decision-making compared to methods using actual vehicle instantaneous speed
Questionnaire calibration alone produces discrepancies (red light jumper proportion 0.69 vs field 0.85), requiring adjustment based on analyzing deviation causes to achieve better agreement

contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

The desired direction model using Weibull distribution with influencing factors (crosswalk geometry, OD movement, pedestrian density) reasonably reflects pedestrian behavior characteristics

claims.claim_text: Pedestrians receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and braking text were the most favored visual interfaces while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAVs poorly even with external features
Pedestrians who intentionally violate traffic rules or get distracted became more cautious with FAVs and appreciated external features

contradictions.task: Predict pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions
contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention across all four models (p<0.001 in all cases)
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit with CFI=0.996, CMIN/df=1.235, RMSEA=0.030
Subjective norm was significant in Model 1 (basic TPB) but became non-significant after descriptive norm was added, indicating descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior (p=0.004 in Model 4), indicating that presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention
Perceived behavioral control was not a significant predictor in any model, likely because none of the respondents were physically impaired and the violation behavior was not challenging enough

contradictions.task: pedestrian crossing task at signalized intersection under three types of mobile phone distraction conditions plus control
contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and then music distraction
All three mobile phone distractions cause pedestrians to initiate crossing later, increase pupil diameter, and reduce scanning frequency and fixation points/times toward traffic signal area
Phone conversation distraction leads to slower crossing speed, fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and less fixation time and average fixation duration on left traffic area
Text distraction causes pedestrians to look left and right less often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment
Eye movement data captured by eye tracker is an effective complement to video data for determining individuals visual attention allocation more accurately

contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649)
Pedestrian age significantly affects red light running propensity: adolescents have lower (b=-0.228) and elderly have higher propensity (b=0.174) compared to adults
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (b=-0.984, odds ratio=0.374)
Social influences strongly affect individual violation decisions: presence of other violators increases propensity (b=1.029) while more pedestrians waiting reduces it (b=-0.394)
Environmental factors including time to green, red time, traffic volume, and number of lanes significantly affect red light running propensity

claims.claim_text: The U-shaped distribution of pedestrian intended waiting times emerges from the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians via statistical unconditioning, showing that a large proportion of pedestrians cross immediately while another large proportion wait for the entire red-man phase

The proposed bounded Pareto mixture model better captures pedestrian waiting time behavior at signalized intersections compared to conventional exponential distribution and its extensions, which cannot adequately model the bounded nature and U-shaped pattern of waiting times
Risk-taking pedestrians have significantly smaller intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians due to their lower effective critical headway and higher sensitivity to excess headway

Younger pedestrians tend to be risk-takers (higher proportion cross immediately) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (higher proportion wait for entire phase); males are more aggressive than females in crossing behavior

contradictions.task: Identify significant factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior
contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians going to work/school have higher red-light running probability than strolling pedestrians (odds ratio = 0.394 for stroll group)
Daytime period shows the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, dusk, and night (OR = 3.326 for daytime vs dawn)
Pedestrians who indicate they will run red lights when in hurry have significantly higher probability of red-light running (odds ratio = 0.299)
Pedestrians who are affected by road facility quality (imperfect facilities) are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio = 0.483 for unaffected group)

claims.claim_text: The proposed model successfully describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, with validation showing maximum relative error less than 10% between simulated and surveyed pedestrian waiting times

Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of traffic lanes and vehicle volume but decreases with pedestrian volume; the number of lanes is the critical factor affecting pedestrian waiting time at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Vehicular delay increases more rapidly than pedestrian waiting time with increasing vehicle volume and pedestrian volume, due to queue formation and platoon departure behavior

Signal control is recommended when vehicular volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h, using 20 seconds delay as LOS B threshold; unsignalized crosswalks should not be set on segments with more than 4 lanes

contradictions.task: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait)
contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system correctly recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation across different noise levels (rn = 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 m)
The system recognizes wait decisions with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds of observation, taking longer than cross decisions due to deceleration cues
The proposed probabilistic model provides confidence scores (probabilities) for states rather than binary classifications, enabling varied vehicle behaviors based on confidence levels
The DBN model successfully integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian physical states, mimicking human decision-making processes
Walking motion recognition maintains 90% accuracy across noise levels, while running motion recognition is more challenging due to overlapping speed distributions with walking

claims.claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian crossing time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model (evidence_chunk_ids: [b6ed7de0-abf9-47b3-8612-4a32489bfee7])

The difference between necessary speed (Vnec) to complete crossing and current speed, conflict area presence, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) indication significantly impact acceleration/deceleration choices (evidence_chunk_ids: [14dbbbc0-5c22-451c-99f2-e80f12dfe430])
